title: Abroma fastuosum
taxonomy: Abroma fastuosum is a member of the family Malvaceae. This family is commonly known as the mallow or hibiscus family, and it includes over 200 genera of flowering plants. Abroma fastuosum is a flowering plant native to tropical regions, specifically in India and Southeast Asia. It is a part of the genus Abroma, which is a group of flowering plants that are typically found in tropical and subtropical regions. The classification of Abroma fastuosum is still a subject of ongoing research and study in the field of botany.
uses: Abroma fastuosum is a plant native to tropical Asia and has been studied for its potential medicinal properties. It has been used in traditional medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. The plant's roots are believed to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Abroma fastuosum has also been researched for its potential to exhibit antioxidant and anticancer activities. Further studies are needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal uses and potential benefits.

These compounds have been found to possess bioactive properties, with potential applications in traditional medicine. side_effects: Abroma fastuosum is known to have various side effects due to its usage in traditional medicine. Abroma fastuosum may cause gastrointestinal issues such as nausea and stomach discomfort in some individuals. Prolonged use of Abroma fastuosum can lead to liver damage and other liver-related problems. Additionally, it may cause allergic reactions, skin irritation, and interact with other medications. It's essential to consult a healthcare professional before using Abroma fastuosum to minimize potential side effects.
intro: Abroma fastuosum is a plant species native to the tropical regions of India and Southeast Asia, commonly known as the "Fastuosum Plant" or "Tongavelli", belonging to the Malvaceae family. The plant has been traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin disorders, due to its anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Abroma fastuosum include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, Abroma fastuosum is often prepared as a decoction or infused in oil to treat various health issues, and it is also used in the form of a paste or powder in Ayurvedic and Unani formulations.
